title: Chapter Fourteen: The Journal
content: Three-seventeen in the morning. I know because I've been watching the clock on Mrs. Grose's kitchen wall for forty-three minutes, waiting for the house to finish its settling, waiting for the last creak of timber to fade into the kind of silence that only a manor at three AM can produce. The kind that presses against your eardrums until you hear your own blood.

The keys hang on their hook behind the spice rack. Mrs. Grose has never moved them. Why would she? No one comes to Bly. No one leaves. The keys are architecture, not utility — just another artifact of a house that keeps its own customs long after the people who made them are gone.
I take them down. My hands do not shake.

I've been planning this for nineteen days. I know which stairs groan and which hold silent. I know that the fourth step from the second landing flexes under weight but the fifth does not. I know the downstairs hallway runs cold near the east wing because the window latch is broken and the draft comes through like a whisper. I know all of this because I have been very, very careful, and careful people do not shake at three in the morning when the house is dead and the twins are sleeping and the only witness is the moon.

The tower key is the smallest on the ring. Brass. Tarnished. Its teeth are worn almost smooth, and when I fit it into the lock I have to press hard and turn with more force than feels right, as if the door has forgotten how to open. As if it's been waiting for permission from someone who no longer exists.
The lock gives.
The door swings inward on silence and dust.

The air in the tower room has weight. It sits on my shoulders, presses against my chest, fills my lungs with something that isn't quite smell and isn't quite memory — a compound of cedar and old paper and the particular staleness of a space where no one has drawn breath in years. I stand in the doorway and let my eyes adjust. Moonlight comes through the window in a long pale rectangle that falls across the floor like a stage spotlight, and everything it touches looks dredged up from deep water.

The wardrobe stands against the far wall. I open it without deciding to. The doors swing outward on hinges that have forgotten how to be quiet, and the smell of cedar intensifies — sharper now, almost medicinal, layered with something underneath that might be mothballs or might be the ghost of cologne. Peter's clothes hang in a row. Shirts, pressed and ordered by color. Trousers, creased down the front as if they've been waiting for an iron that never came. A cashmere sweater, dark green, folded over the wooden bar with the kind of precision that speaks of habit, of a man who arranged his things each evening with the unconscious ritual of someone who believed he would need them again in the morning.
I touch the sleeve of a shirt. White cotton. Stiff with disuse. My fingers leave a dark line in the dust and I pull my hand back as if I've been burned.

The books are on the shelf beside the wardrobe. Philosophy, mostly — Wittgenstein, Kierkegaard, a creased paperback of *The Stranger* with a spine cracked in three places. I pull one down and open it. Peter's handwriting fills the margins. A beautiful, slanting script that leans forward as if it's rushing to catch up with its own thoughts. Notes upon notes, counter-arguments, small furious rebuttals written in the white spaces between paragraphs. He argued with everything he read. He couldn't help it.
I know that feeling.
I put the book back.

The chair faces the window. A reading chair, leather, cracked across the seat where the cushion has dried and split. It's positioned at an angle that suggests its owner liked to look up from his book and see the lake — that wide grey expanse that Bly keeps at the end of every vista like a full stop. The last thing he saw. The last thing he chose to see, before whatever happened on that lake took him.
And then: the desk.
The photograph is in a silver frame.

It sits in the center of the desk as if it was placed there deliberately, positioned so that anyone sitting in Peter's chair would see it. The silver has tarnished to a dull charcoal, and the glass over the photograph is filmed with dust so thick that the image underneath looks submerged, drowning in grey.
I pick the frame up. I blow the dust away. It rises in a small cloud and settles on my sleeve, on the desk, on my fingers. The photograph sharpens.
Peter.

Young. Dark-haired. Standing in front of Bly Manor with the east wing visible over his left shoulder, which means the photograph was taken from the garden path, which means someone stood there and pointed a camera at him and he smiled — not broadly, not for show, but with the corner of his mouth, the way people smile when they're caught off-guard by happiness.
His arm is around a woman.

She has dark hair. Dark eyes. She's wearing a coat — a long dark coat with wooden toggles, and I know that coat. I've seen it in a polaroid, stuffed in a box in a storage unit in Lewisham, the only photograph of my mother that survived her. The coat she wore the winter before she left. The coat she was wearing when she told my grandmother she was going away and not coming back.
The woman in the photograph is my mother.

The room shifts. Not the walls — the walls stay where they are. But something behind them moves, some load-bearing structure in the architecture of what I've believed, and the floor tilts under my feet in a way that has nothing to do with the tower's foundation.
She was here.
My mother was here. At Bly. With Peter Quint. Her arm around his waist, his arm around her shoulders, standing in front of this house as if they belonged to it. As if Bly was theirs.
I knew it.

I came here knowing it. I spent ten years tracking the money — the payments from an account in Peter's name to an address in Lewisham, monthly, regular, stopping the month after he died. I found the letters she never sent, hidden in a shoebox under her bed after she disappeared for good. I built the chain of evidence link by patient link until the only conclusion left was the one I'd been refusing to reach: my mother had been Peter Quint's lover, and I was his daughter.
But knowing is not the same as seeing.
Seeing is different.

Seeing is the coat. Seeing is her face next to his. Seeing is the way she leans into him, unguarded, a version of my mother I never knew — a woman who let herself lean. Who let herself be held by a man in front of a camera.
My fingers are pressed flat against the glass. My fingertips have left prints in the dust. I lift my hand and look at the grey residue on my skin, and something breaks.

Not loudly. Not the way it breaks in books, with crying and screaming and the rending of garments. It breaks the way an ice shelf breaks — slowly, a crack that's been forming for years, running deeper than anyone could see, and then the final pressure that splits it apart in a silence so vast it swallows sound itself.

I sit down on the floor. I don't choose to sit. My legs simply stop performing the function of legs and I am on the floor with the photograph in my lap and my back against the desk and the moonlight finding me where I sit, and I am not crying because crying would require a kind of participation I can't afford, but I am not okay. I have not been okay since I was sixteen years old and my mother walked out of our flat in Lewisham and didn't come back, and I have been performing okay for ten years the way a patient performs wellness for a doctor, and right now in this tower room at three in the morning the performance ends.
I hold the photograph.

I don't know how long I sit there. Long enough for the moon to shift its angle through the window. Long enough for the cold of the floor to seep through my clothes and press against my spine like a rebuke.
Then I remember why I came.
The floorboard by the window creaks.

I've been stepping over it for nineteen days on the rare occasions I've passed the tower door, but tonight I press down on it deliberately, feeling the give, the slight bow of old wood that's come loose from its nail. Jessel wrote about it in the margins of the *Wittgenstein*: *under the board that creaks by the window*. A message hidden inside another message, the way all important things at Bly are hidden — nested, encrypted, waiting for someone with the patience to read between the annotations.
I slide my fingers into the gap. The board lifts. Beneath it, pressed flat against the joist, is a notebook.

Not the *Wittgenstein*. Not the frantic, desperate margin-notes that Jessel left where anyone could find them, the ones that read like a woman losing her mind in real time. This is something else. A proper journal — black cover, stitched binding, the kind you buy at a stationer's when you intend to write something worth preserving. The pages have yellowed but the ink is still dark. Rebecca Jessel wrote in a small, neat hand, nothing like the frantic scrawl of the annotations. She took her time with this. She wanted it to last.
I open it by the window, where the moonlight is brightest.

*October 3rd. Peter taught the children the Game tonight. I watched from the doorway. He called it a thought experiment — a way of seeing, he said. The beautiful lie that reveals the ugly truth. Miles understood immediately. Flora took longer, but when she got it, her face changed in a way I can only describe as recognition. As if she'd been waiting for someone to name something she already knew.*

*October 15th. The Game is accelerating. Peter drives it. He plays against the twins and they play against each other and I am beginning to understand that the Game is not a game at all, or not only a game — it is a way of staying inside Bly without being consumed by it. Peter says the house wants things from you. The Game is how you refuse. The Game is how you choose what you give and what you keep.*

*November 2nd. I'm playing now. The third player. Peter says I'm better at it than he is, which is a lie, or a move, or both — with Peter, those categories are the same thing. But I am good. I can feel the architecture of it in my mind, the way each play opens a dozen possible futures. It's like chess, except the board changes and the pieces lie and you can win by convincing your opponent that you've already lost.*

*December 19th. I found the receipts. Western Union. Monthly transfers to an address in Lewisham, southeast London. A woman's name. A cleaning woman. I asked Peter about it and he went still — that particular stillness I've learned to read, the one that means I've touched something real. He didn't deny it. He said: "She was the only honest person I ever knew. And I owed her." He owed her. A cleaning woman in Lewisham.*

*December 20th. He has a child. I know it the way I know all things at Bly — not because anyone told me, but because the shape of the omission tells me. The money. The stillness. The way he looks toward the lake sometimes as if he's calculating a distance that has nothing to do with water. He was sending money to a woman in London. A cleaning woman. He had a child. The child would come for him someday — I know she will. And when she does, the game will change.*

*January 7th. I am leaving. Not the way Peter left — not into the lake, not into the cold. I'm leaving the way you leave a house that was never yours: through the front door, quietly, with what I can carry. But I'm leaving this behind. I'm leaving it because I know — the way I know all things at Bly — that she will come. Peter's daughter. She has his mind. She'll find the annotations. She'll find the board that creaks. She'll find this.*
*To the woman who comes after me: Peter's daughter will come to Bly. She has his eyes. When you meet her, tell her —*
*The game was never the point. The point was always the escape.*
I read the last line three times.
The game was never the point. The point was always the escape.

I read it again. I hold the journal in both hands and the words don't change because they were written to stay the same, written for me, addressed to me, by a woman I've never met who knew I would stand in this room and read them in moonlight.

She predicted me. Not with mysticism or prophecy — with logic. The same logic I used to find Bly. She traced the chain from Peter to Lewisham to the child, and she knew that child would grow up, and she knew that child would come looking, because the child of Peter Quint would have Peter's mind and Peter's will and Peter's relentless need to see the thing behind the pattern.
She has his eyes.
I close the journal. I hold it against my chest. In my lap, the photograph watches me — my mother's face and my father's face, pressed together under glass, patient as only the photographed can be.
I am not just his investigator. I am his legacy. The Game is not something I found. It is something I inherited.
Something I was built for.

The tower room is very quiet. The kind of quiet that has a sound — the faint electrical hum of a house holding its breath, the tick of wood cooling in the night. I stand. My legs work again. I put the photograph in my bag. I put the journal in my bag. I replace the floorboard. I close the wardrobe doors.
I turn to the window.

The lake is silver in the moonlight, flat and still as a mirror laid on its back. The far shore is a dark line against the sky. The lawn slopes down from the manor to the water's edge in a long pale tongue of grass.
And there, by the lake, a figure stands.
Woman-shaped. Dark-haired. Standing at the water's edge with her back to the house, looking out at the lake as if she's waiting for something to surface.

I press my hand against the cold glass. The temperature shocks me — real, material, undeniable. The glass is freezing and my palm sticks to it and the condensation from my breath clouds the window and I wipe it clear with my sleeve and the figure is still there.
I can't see her face from this height. She's too far away, too small, just a shape with dark hair against the silver water.

But Jessel knew who she'd be. Jessel wrote it down in her careful hand and left it under the board that creaks by the window, and maybe Jessel was wrong — maybe the figure by the lake is just a trick of moonlight and shadow and the overactive pattern-recognition of a mind that has been solving puzzles since it was old enough to think.
Or maybe she's exactly who Jessel said.
Maybe she's been waiting for me.

I stand at the window of my father's room with my mother's photograph and my predecessor's journal and I look down at the dark-haired figure by the lake, and I think: *the point was always the escape*.
What escape?
Whose?

The figure doesn't move. I don't move. Between us, the moonlight makes a bridge of silver across the dark lawn, and the house holds its silence, and somewhere below me — locked away behind doors the twins can reach and I cannot — the Game goes on.
I have what I came for. I have more than I came for.

I don't leave the tower room. Not yet. I stand at the window and I wait, because Jessel left me the journal and Peter left me the Game and my mother left me nothing but the coat on her back, and there is a figure by the lake who might be a ghost or a memory or a woman I haven't met yet, and I need to know which.
The journal is warm against my chest where I hold it.
*The point was always the escape.*
Down below, the figure turns.
Just slightly. Just enough. Still no face from this height. But the gesture is unmistakable — an acknowledgment, a recognition, a truth arriving late but arriving nonetheless.
She knows I'm here.
I hold the cold glass and I hold the journal and I do not look away.
